Clinical_Definitions.definition: any therapeutic, prophylactic or diagnostic agent

an individual study of any therapeutic, prophylactic or diagnostic agent conducted in patients, in which the clinical and statistical description, presentations, and analyses are integrated into a single report, incorporating tables and figures into the main text of the report, or at the end of the text, and with appendices containing the protocol, sample case report forms, investigator related information, information related to the test drugs/investigational products including active control/comparators, technical statistical documentation, related publications, patient data listings, and technical statistical details such as derivations, computations, analyses, and computer output etc.

any untoward medical occurrence that at any dose: results in death, is life-threatening, requires inpatient hospitalisation or prolongation of existing hospitalisation, results in persistent or significant disability/incapacity, or is a congenital anomaly/birth defect.

marked haematological and other laboratory abnormalities and any adverse events that led to an intervention, including withdrawal of drug treatment, dose reduction or significant additional concomitant therapy.
those not seen at baseline and those that worsened even if present at baseline.
a laboratory measurement or physical measurement or sign that is not a direct measure of clinical benefit

a single study under a common protocol, involving several centres (e.g., clinics, practices, hospitals) where the data collected are intended to be analysed as a whole (as opposed to a post-hoc decision to combine data or results from separate studies).
the degree of inferiority of the therapy the study was intended to show was not exceeded.
Independent Ethics Committee
Institutional Review Board
Good Clinical Practices
Case Report Form
International Conference on Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Registration of Pharmaceuticals for Human Use
Contract Research Organisation
Any unfavourable medical occurrence in a trial participant administered the investigational product. The adverse event does not necessarily have a causal relationship with the treatment.

In the pre-approval clinical experience with a new investigational product or its new usages (particularly as the therapeutic dose(s) may not be established): unfavourable and unintended responses, such as a sign (e.g., laboratory results), symptom or disease related to any dose of a medicinal product where a causal relationship between a medicinal product and an adverse event is a reasonable possibility. The level of certainty about the relatedness of the adverse drug reaction to an investigational product will vary. If the ADR is suspected to be medicinal product-related with a high level of certainty, it should be included in the reference safety information (RSI) and/or the Investigator’s Brochure (IB). For marketed medicinal products: a response to a drug that is noxious and unintended and that occurs at doses normally used in humans for prophylaxis, diagnosis or therapy of diseases or for modification of physiological function.

Any unfavourable medical occurrence that is considered serious at any dose if it: Results in death, Is life-threatening, Requires inpatient hospitalisation or prolongation of existing hospitalisation, Results in persistent or significant disability/incapacity, or Is a congenital anomaly/birth defect. An important medical event that may not be immediately life-threatening or result in death or hospitalisation, that may jeopardise the participant or that may require intervention to prevent serious outcomes (see ICH E2A and E19) should generally be considered as serious.

an adverse reaction that meets three criteria: suspected, unexpected and serious. Suspected: There is a reasonable possibility that the drug caused the adverse drug reaction. Unexpected: An adverse reaction, the nature or severity of which is not consistent with the applicable product information (e.g., Investigator’s Brochure or alternative documents according to applicable regulatory requirements; see RSI). Serious: See above for SAE.

A document or set of documents describing the details of any arrangements on delegation or transfer, distribution and/or sharing of activities and, if appropriate, on financial matters between two or more parties. This could be in the form of a contract. The protocol may serve as the basis of an agreement.
Any law(s) and regulation(s) addressing the conduct of clinical trials of investigational products.
Affirmative agreement of a minor to participate in clinical trial. The absence of expression of agreement or disagreement should not be interpreted as assent.

A systematic and independent examination of trial-related activities and records performed by the sponsor, service provider (including contract research organisation (CRO)) or institution to determine whether the evaluated trial-related activities were conducted and the data were recorded, analysed and accurately reported according to the protocol, applicable standard operating procedures (SOPs), Good Clinical Practice (GCP) and the applicable regulatory requirement(s).
A declaration of confirmation by the auditor that an audit has taken place.
A record describing the conduct and outcome of the audit.

Metadata records that allow the appropriate evaluation of the course of events by capturing details on actions (manual or automated) performed relating to information and data collection and, where applicable, to activities in computerised systems. The audit trail should show activities, initial entry and changes to data fields or records, by whom, when and, where applicable, why. In computerised systems, the audit trail should be secure, computer-generated and time stamped.

A procedure in which one or more parties to the trial are kept unaware of the treatment assignment(s). Single-blinding usually refers to the participant(s) being unaware, and double-blinding usually refers to the participant(s) and investigator(s) and, if appropriate, other investigator site staff or sponsor staff being unaware of the treatment assignment(s).
A data acquisition tool designed to record protocol-required information to be reported by the investigator to the sponsor on each trial participant (see Data Acquisition Tool).

A copy (irrespective of the type of media used) of the original record that has been verified (i.e., by a dated signature or by generation through a validated process) to have the same information as the original, including relevant metadata, where applicable.

Any interventional investigation in human participants intended to discover or verify the clinical, pharmacological and/or other pharmacodynamic effects of an investigational product(s); and/or to identify any adverse reactions to an investigational product(s); and/or to study absorption, distribution, metabolism and excretion of an investigational product(s) with the object of ascertaining its safety and/or efficacy.

A documented description of a trial of any investigational product conducted in human participants, in which the clinical and statistical description, presentations and analyses are fully integrated into a single report (see ICH E3 Structure and Content of Clinical Study Reports).
An investigational or authorised medicinal product (i.e., active control), placebo or standard of care used as a reference in a clinical trial.
Adherence to the trial-related requirements, GCP requirements and the applicable regulatory requirements.
Prevention of disclosure to other than authorised individuals of a sponsor's proprietary information or of a participant's identity or their confidential information.
An investigator assigned the responsibility for the coordination of investigators at different investigator sites participating in a multicentre trial.

A process of establishing and documenting that the specified requirements of a computerised system can be consistently fulfilled from design until decommissioning of the system or transition to a new system. The approach to validation should be based on a risk assessment that takes into consideration the intended use of the system and the potential of the system to affect trial participant protection and the reliability of trial results.
See Service Provider.

A paper or electronic tool designed to collect data and associated metadata from a data originator in a clinical trial according to the protocol and to report the data to the sponsor. The data originator may be a human (e.g., the participant or trial staff), a machine (e.g., wearables and sensors) or a computer system from which the electronic transfer of data from one system to another has been undertaken (e.g., extraction of data from an electronic health record or laboratory system). Examples of DATs include but are not limited to CRFs, interactive response technologies (IRTs), clinical outcome assessments (COAs), including patient-reported outcomes (PROs) and wearable devices, irrespective of the media used.

Data integrity includes the degree to which data fulfil key criteria of being attributable, legible, contemporaneous, original, accurate, complete, secure and reliable such that data are fit for purpose.

Permission to examine, analyse and verify records that are important to the evaluation of a clinical trial and may be performed on-site or remotely. Any party (e.g., domestic and foreign regulatory authorities, sponsor's monitors and auditors) with direct access should take reasonable precautions within the constraints of the applicable regulatory requirement(s) to maintain the confidentiality of participants' identities and their data and sponsor's proprietary information.

Essential records are the documents and data (and relevant metadata), in any format, associated with a clinical trial that facilitate the ongoing management of the trial and collectively allow the evaluation of the methods used, the factors affecting a trial and the actions taken during the trial conduct to determine the reliability of the trial results produced and the verification that the trial was conducted in accordance with GCP and applicable regulatory requirements (see Appendix C).

A standard for the planning, initiating, performing, recording, oversight, evaluation, analysis and reporting of clinical trials that provides assurance that the data and reported results are reliable and that the rights, safety and well-being of trial participants are protected.

A person who is independent of the trial who cannot be unfairly influenced by people involved with the trial, who attends the informed consent process if the participant or the participant's legally acceptable representative cannot read, and who reads the informed consent form and any other documented information supplied or read to the participant and/or their legally acceptable representative.

An independent data monitoring committee (e.g., data safety monitoring board) that may be established by the sponsor to assess at intervals the progress of a clinical trial, the safety and relevant efficacy data, and to recommend to the sponsor whether to continue, modify or stop a trial.

A process by which a participant or their legally acceptable representative voluntarily confirms their willingness to participate in a trial after having been informed and been provided with the opportunity to discuss all aspects of the trial that are relevant to the participant's decision to participate. Varied approaches to the provision of information and the discussion about the trial can be used. This may include, for example, providing text in different formats, images and videos and using telephone or video conferencing with investigator site staff. Informed consent is documented by means of a written (paper or electronic), signed and dated informed consent form. Obtaining consent remotely may be considered when appropriate.

The act by a regulatory authority(ies) of conducting an official review of documents, facilities, records and any other resources that are deemed by the authority(ies) to be related to the clinical trial and that may be accessed at the investigator site, at the sponsor's and/or service provider's (including CRO's) facilities, or at other establishments deemed appropriate by the regulatory authority(ies). Some aspects of the inspection may be conducted remotely.
Any public or private entity or agency or medical or dental organisation in whose remit clinical trials are conducted.

An independent body (a review board or a committee, institutional, regional, national or supranational) constituted of medical professionals and non-medical members whose responsibility it is to ensure the protection of the rights, safety and well-being of human participants involved in a trial and to provide public assurance of that protection by, among other things, reviewing and approving/providing favourable opinion on the trial protocol, the suitability of the investigator(s), the facilities, and the methods and material to be used in obtaining and documenting informed consent of the trial participants. The legal status, composition, function, operations and regulatory requirements pertaining to IRBs/IECs may differ among countries but should allow the IRB/IEC to act in agreement with GCP as described in this guideline.
A report of intermediate results and their evaluation based on analyses performed during the course of a trial.

A pharmaceutical form of an active ingredient or placebo being tested or used as a reference in a clinical trial, including a product with a marketing authorisation when used or assembled (formulated or packaged) in a way different from the approved form, or when used for an unapproved indication, or when used to gain further information about an approved use. Investigational products should be considered synonymous with drugs, medicines, medicinal products, vaccines and biological products.

A person responsible for the conduct of the clinical trial, including the trial participants for whom that person has responsibility during the conduct of the trial. If a trial is conducted by a team of individuals, the investigator is the responsible leader of the team and may be called the principal investigator. Where an investigator/institution is referenced in this guideline, it describes expectations that may be applicable to the investigator and/or the institution in some regions. Where required by the applicable regulatory requirements, the "investigator" should be read as "investigator and/or the institution."
A compilation of the clinical and nonclinical data on the investigational product(s) that is relevant to the study of the investigational product(s) in human participants (see Appendix A).
The location(s) where trial-related activities are conducted and/or coordinated under the investigator's/institution's oversight.

An individual or juridical or other body authorised under applicable law to consent, on behalf of a prospective participant, to the participant’s participation in the clinical trial. When a legally acceptable representative provides consent on behalf of a prospective participant, activities related to the consenting process (and re-consent, if applicable) and, where relevant, activities associated with the withdrawal of consent described in this guideline are applicable to the participant’s legally acceptable representative.

The contextual information required to understand a given data element. Metadata is structured information that describes, explains or otherwise makes it easier to retrieve, use or manage data. For the purpose of this guideline, relevant metadata are those needed to allow the appropriate evaluation of the trial conduct.

The act of overseeing the progress of a clinical trial and of ensuring that the clinical trial is conducted, recorded and reported in accordance with the protocol, SOPs, GCP and the applicable regulatory requirement(s).
A document that describes the strategy, methods, responsibilities and requirements for monitoring the trial.
A documented report following site and/or centralised monitoring activities.
A clinical trial conducted according to a single protocol but at more than one investigator site.
Biomedical studies not performed on human participants.

A document that describes the objective(s), design, methodology, statistical considerations and organisation of a trial. The protocol usually also gives the background and rationale for the trial, but these could be provided in other protocol referenced documents. Throughout the ICH GCP Guideline, the term “protocol” refers to protocol and protocol amendments.
A documented description of a change(s) to a protocol.

All those planned and systematic actions that are established to ensure that the trial is performed and the data are generated, documented (recorded) and reported in compliance with GCP and the applicable regulatory requirement(s).
The operational techniques and activities undertaken to verify that the requirements for quality of the trial-related activities have been fulfilled.
The process of deliberately including an element of chance when assigning participants to groups that receive different treatments in order to reduce bias.

Contains a cumulative list of ADRs that are expected for the investigational product being administered to participants in a clinical trial. The RSI is included in the Investigator’s Brochure or alternative documents according to applicable regulatory requirements. Refer to ICH E2F Development Safety Update Report for more information about RSI.

Bodies having the power to regulate, including those that review submitted protocols and clinical data and those that conduct inspections. These bodies are sometimes referred to as competent authorities.
A person or organisation (commercial, academic or other) providing a service used by either the sponsor or the investigator to fulfil trial-related activities.

A unique mark, symbol or entry executed, adopted or authorised by an individual, in accordance with applicable regulatory requirements and/or practice to show expression of will and allow authentication of the signatory (i.e., establish a high degree of certainty that a record was signed by the claimed signatory). A signature may be physical or electronic.

Original documents or data (which includes relevant metadata) or certified copies of the original documents or data, irrespective of the media used. This may include trial participants’ medical/health records/notes/charts; data provided/entered by trial participants (e.g., electronic patient-reported outcomes (ePROs)); healthcare professionals’ records from pharmacies, laboratories and other facilities involved in the clinical trial; and data from automated instruments, such as wearables and sensors.

An individual, company, institution or organisation that takes responsibility for the initiation, management and arrangement of the financing of a clinical trial. A clinical trial may have one or several sponsors where permitted under regulatory requirements. All sponsors have the responsibilities of a sponsor set out in this guideline. In accordance with applicable regulatory requirements, sponsors may decide in a documented agreement setting out their respective responsibilities. Where the documented agreement does not specify to which sponsor a given responsibility is attributed, that responsibility lies with all sponsors.

An individual who both initiates and conducts, alone or with others, a clinical trial, and under whose immediate direction the investigational product is administered to, dispensed to or used by a participant. The term does not include any person other than an individual (e.g., the term does not include a corporation or an agency). The obligations of a sponsor-investigator include both those of a sponsor and those of an investigator.
Detailed, documented instructions to achieve uniformity of the performance of a specific activity.

Any individual member of the clinical trial team designated and under the oversight of the investigator to perform significant trial-related procedures and/or to make important trial-related decisions (e.g., associates, residents, research fellows).

An individual who participates in a clinical trial who is expected to receive the investigational product(s) or as a control. In this guideline, trial participant and participant are used interchangeably.

A unique identifier assigned to each trial participant to protect the participant's identity and used in lieu of the participant's name when the investigator reports adverse events and/or other trial-related data.

Individuals whose willingness to volunteer in a clinical trial may be unduly influenced by the expectation, whether justified or not, of benefits associated with participation or of a retaliatory response from senior members of a hierarchy in case of refusal to participate. Examples are members of a group with a hierarchical structure, such as medical, pharmacy, dental and nursing students; subordinate hospital and laboratory personnel; employees of the pharmaceutical industry; members of the armed forces; and persons kept in detention. Other vulnerable participants may include persons in nursing homes, unemployed or impoverished persons, patients in emergency situations, ethnic minority groups, homeless persons, nomads, refugees, minors and those incapable of giving consent.

Global_Writing_Constraints: The objective of this guideline is to allow the compilation of a single core clinical study report acceptable to all regulatory authorities of the ICH regions.
Regulatory authority specific additions will consist of modules to be considered as appendices, available upon request according to regional regulatory requirements.
The clinical and statistical description, presentations, and analyses must be integrated into a single report.
Tables and figures must be incorporated into the main text of the report or at the end of the text.
The integrated full report of a study should not be derived by simply joining a separate clinical and statistical report.
The report must be complete, free from ambiguity, well organised, and easy to review.
Provide a clear explanation of how the critical design features of the study were chosen.
Provide enough information on the plan, methods, and conduct of the study so that there is no ambiguity in how the study was carried out.

The report with its appendices must provide enough individual patient data, including demographic and baseline data, and details of analytical methods, to allow replication of critical analyses by authorities.
All analyses, tables, and figures must carry, in text or as part of the table, clear identification of the set of patients from which they were generated.
If an abbreviated report is submitted, a full description of safety aspects must be included.
If an abbreviated report is submitted, it must contain enough detail of design and results to allow the regulatory authority to determine whether a full report is needed.
Clarify features of the study that are not well-described in the protocol.
Identify ways in which the study as conducted differed from the protocol.
Discuss the statistical methods and analyses used to account for deviations from the planned protocol.

The full integrated report of the individual study should include the most detailed discussion of individual adverse events or laboratory abnormalities, but these should usually be reexamined as part of an overall safety analysis of all available data in any application.
Describe demographic and other potentially predictive characteristics of the study population.

Present data for demographic (e.g., age, sex, race, weight) and other (e.g., renal or hepatic function) subgroups so that possible differences in efficacy or safety can be identified, where the study is large enough.
Subgroup responses should usually be examined in the larger database used in the overall analysis.
Data listings that are part of the report must be readily usable by the reviewer.
If a single listing sacrifices clarity due to an excess of data, overuse of symbols, or too small displays, it is preferable to produce several listings.

Data must be presented at different levels of detail: overall summary figures and tables for important demographic, efficacy, and safety variables may be placed in the text; other summary figures, tables, and listings for demographic, efficacy, and safety variables should be provided in Section 14; individual patient data for specified groups of patients should be provided as listings in Appendix 16.2; and all individual patient data (archival listings requested only in the US) should be provided in Appendix 16.4.
In any table, figure, or data listing, estimated or derived values, if used, must be identified in a conspicuous fashion.
Detailed explanations must be provided as to how estimated or derived values were obtained and what underlying assumptions were made.
Each report should consider all described topics unless clearly not relevant.
The specific sequence and grouping of topics may be changed if alternatives are more logical for a particular study.
The numbering of appendices should be adapted accordingly if some data are specific requirements of individual regulatory authorities and submitted as appropriate.
For very large trials, contact with regulatory authorities to discuss an appropriate report format is encouraged.
The provisions of this guideline should be used in conjunction with other ICH guidelines.
The synopsis must include numerical data to illustrate results, not just text or p-values.
Abbreviated terms must be spelled out and the abbreviation indicated in parentheses at first appearance in the text.
In every section of the report, a clear distinction between conditions (procedures) planned in the protocol and amendments or additions must be made.
The timing of changes relative to blind breaking and availability of outcome results must be well characterised.

Even if the applicant's proposed primary analysis is based on a reduced subset of patients with data, an additional analysis using all randomised (or otherwise entered) patients with any on-treatment data must be performed for any trial intended to establish efficacy.
The analysis must show the size (point estimate) of the difference between treatments, the associated confidence interval, and, where utilized, the results of hypothesis testing.
If categories are newly created (i.e., not in the statistical plan), the basis for them must be explained.

If critical measurements or assessments of efficacy or safety outcomes were made by more than one party, overall differences between the ratings must be shown, and each patient having disparate assessments must be identified.
The assessments used must be clear in all analyses.
The statistical analysis used must be described for clinical and statistical reviewers in the text of the report.
Any changes in the analysis made after blind-breaking must be identified.

If covariates or methods used in analyses differed from those planned in the protocol, the differences must be explained, and where possible and relevant, the results of planned analyses should also be presented.
Procedures for dealing with missing data, e.g., use of estimated or derived data, must be described.
Detailed explanation must be provided as to how such estimations or derivations were done and what underlying assumptions were made.

All interim analyses, formal or informal, pre-planned or ad hoc, by any study participant, sponsor staff member, or data monitoring group must be described in full, even if the treatment groups were not identified.
The need for statistical adjustment because of interim analyses must be addressed.
Any operating instructions or procedures used for interim analyses must be described.
Data monitoring without code-breaking should also be described, even if this kind of monitoring is considered to cause no increase in type I error.
Individual centre results should be presented where appropriate, e.g., when centres have sufficient numbers of patients to make such analysis potentially valuable.
The possibility of qualitative or quantitative treatment-by-centre interaction should be explored.
Any extreme or opposite results among centres must be noted and discussed, considering such possibilities as differences in study conduct, patient characteristics, or clinical settings.
Treatment comparison must include analyses that allow for centre differences with respect to response.
If appropriate, demographic, baseline, and post-baseline data, as well as efficacy data, should be presented by centre, even though the combined analysis is the primary one.

If there was more than one primary endpoint, more than one analysis of a particular endpoint, multiple treatment groups, or subsets of the patient population being examined, the statistical analysis must reflect awareness of this and either explain the statistical adjustment used for type I error criteria or give reasons why it was considered unnecessary.
It is generally advantageous to demonstrate robustness of the principal trial conclusions with respect to alternative choices of patient populations for analysis.
Any substantial differences resulting from the choice of patient population for analysis must be the subject of explicit discussion.

If an active control study is intended to show equivalence, the analysis must show the confidence interval for the comparison between the two agents for critical end points and the relation of that interval to the prespecified degree of inferiority that would be considered unacceptable.
If the study size permits, important demographic or baseline value-defined subgroups should be examined for unusually large or small responses, and the results presented.
If subgroup analyses were not carried out because the study was too small, this must be noted.
Where there is a prior hypothesis of a differential effect in a particular subgroup, this hypothesis and its assessment must be part of the planned statistical analysis.
The study report must indicate what material is included as an appendix, what is in the more extensive archival case report tabulations (if required), and what is available on request.
When the dose in each patient can vary, the actual doses received by patients must be shown, and individual patient's doses must be tabulated.
Drug concentration information, if available, must be tabulated, analysed in pharmacokinetic terms, and, if possible, related to response.
In all tabulations and analyses, events associated with both test drug and control treatment must be displayed.

In presenting adverse events, it is important both to display the original terms used by the investigator and to attempt to group related events (i.e., events that probably represent the same phenomena) so that the true occurrence rate is not obscured.
A standard adverse reaction/events dictionary can be used to group related events.
It is assumed that all patients entered into treatment who received at least one dose of the treatment are included in the safety analysis; if not, an explanation must be provided.
Summary tables of adverse events must include changes in vital signs and any laboratory changes considered serious adverse events or other significant adverse events.
Even when a causality assessment is used, adverse event tables must include all adverse events, whether or not considered drug related, including events thought to represent intercurrent illnesses.
To enable analysis and evaluation of data in adverse event tables, each patient having each adverse event must be identified.
Any abbreviations and codes in listings must be clearly explained at the beginning of the listing or, preferably, on each page.

The significance of deaths, other serious adverse events, and other significant adverse events leading to withdrawal, dose reduction, or institution of concomitant therapy must be assessed with respect to the safety of the test drug/investigational product.

Particular attention must be paid to whether any deaths, serious adverse events, or significant adverse events may represent a previously unsuspected important adverse effect of the test drug/investigational product.
For serious adverse events of particular importance, life table or similar analyses may be used to show their relation to time on test drug/investigational product and to assess their risk over time.
For each laboratory analysis, comparison of treatment and control groups must be carried out, as appropriate and compatible with study size.
Normal laboratory ranges must be given for each laboratory analysis.
Any decisions to exclude certain abnormal laboratory values from further analysis must be clearly explained.
The complete list of laboratory values (provided or available on request) must identify every abnormal value.
Clinically significant changes (defined by the applicant) must be discussed.
When toxicity grading scales are used (e.g., WHO, NCI), changes graded as severe must be discussed regardless of seriousness.
An analysis of clinically significant changes, together with a recapitulation of discontinuations due to laboratory measurements, must be provided for each parameter.

The significance of laboratory changes and likely relation to treatment must be assessed, e.g., by analysis of relationship to dose, drug concentration, disappearance on continued therapy, positive dechallenge, positive rechallenge, and nature of concomitant therapy.
Vital signs, other physical findings, and other observations related to safety must be analysed and presented similarly to laboratory variables.

If there is evidence of a drug effect, any dose-response or drug concentration-response relationship or relationship to patient variables must be identified, and the clinical relevance of the observation described.
Particular attention must be given to changes not evaluated as efficacy variables and to those considered to be adverse events.

The overall safety evaluation of the test drug(s)/investigational product(s) must be reviewed, with particular attention to events resulting in changes of dose or need for concomitant medication, serious adverse events, events resulting in withdrawal, and deaths.

Any patients or patient groups at increased risk must be identified, with particular attention to potentially vulnerable patients (e.g., children, pregnant women, frail elderly, people with marked abnormalities of drug metabolism or excretion).
The implication of the safety evaluation for the possible uses of the drug must be described.
The efficacy and safety results of the study and the relationship of risks and benefit must be briefly summarised and discussed, referring to tables, figures, and sections as needed.
The presentation should not simply repeat the description of results nor introduce new results.

The discussion and conclusions must clearly identify any new or unexpected findings, comment on their significance, and discuss any potential problems such as inconsistencies between related measures.
The clinical relevance and importance of the results must also be discussed in light of other existing data.
Any specific benefits or special precautions required for individual subjects or at-risk groups and any implications for the conduct of future studies must be identified.
Alternatively, such discussions may be reserved for summaries of safety and efficacy referring to the entire dossier (integrated summaries).
Figures should be used to visually summarise important results or clarify results not easily understood from tables.
Important demographic, efficacy, and safety data should be presented in summary figures or tables in the text of the report.

If important demographic, efficacy, and safety data become obtrusive due to size or number, they should be presented in Section 14, cross-referenced to the text, along with supportive or additional figures, tables, or listings.

References must be given in accordance with the internationally accepted standards of the 1979 Vancouver Declaration on 'Uniform Requirements for Manuscripts Submitted to Biomedical Journals' or the system used in 'Chemical Abstracts'.
Section 16 must be prefaced by a full list of all appendices available for the study report.
The applicant must clearly indicate those appendices that are submitted with the report.
Appendices available on request must be finalised by the time of filing of the submission.
The statistical model underlying the analysis must be presented precisely and completely, using references if necessary.
A statement of the clinical claim tested in precise statistical terms (e.g., null and alternative hypotheses) must be provided.
The statistical methods applied to estimate effects, construct confidence intervals, etc., must be included, with literature references where appropriate.
The assumptions underlying the statistical methods must be stated.
It must be shown, insofar as statistically reasonable, that the data satisfy crucial assumptions, especially when necessary to confirm the validity of an inference.

When extensive statistical analyses have been performed, it is essential to consider the extent to which analyses were planned prior to data availability and how bias was avoided in choosing the particular analysis used for conclusions.
If data transformation was performed, a rationale for the choice of data transformation along with interpretation of treatment effects based on transformed data must be provided.

A discussion of the appropriateness of the choice of statistical procedure and the validity of statistical conclusions will guide the regulatory authority's statistical reviewer in determining whether reanalysis of data is needed.

The test statistic, its sampling distribution under the null hypothesis, its value, significance level (p-value), and intermediate summary data must be provided in a format that enables verification of results.
P-values must be designated as one- or two-tailed.
The rationale for using a one-tailed test must be provided.

Documentation of a two-sample t-test must consist of the t-statistic value, associated degrees of freedom, p-value, two sample sizes, mean and variance for each sample, and pooled estimate of variance.

Documentation of multi-centre studies analysed by analysis of variance techniques must include, at a minimum, an analysis of variance table with terms for centres, treatments, their interaction, error, and total.

For crossover designs, documentation must include information regarding sequences, patients within sequences, baselines at the start of each period, washouts and length of washouts, dropouts during each period, treatments, periods, treatment by period interaction, error, and total.
For each source of variation (aside from total), the table must contain degrees of freedom, sum of squares, mean square, appropriate F-test, p-value, and expected mean square.

Intermediate summary data must display demographic and response data, averaged or otherwise summarised, for each centre-by-treatment combination (or other design characteristic) at each observation time.

Data listings (tabulations) of patient data utilized by the sponsor for statistical analyses and tables supporting conclusions and major findings must be included in the report of each controlled clinical study.
These data listings are necessary for the regulatory authority's statistical review, and the sponsor may be asked to supply these patient data listings in a computer-readable form.
Clinical trial results must be reliable.
Communication of trial results to participants should be objective and non-promotional.
The clinical trial report must describe the quality management approach implemented in the trial (see ICH E3 Structure and Content of Clinical Study Reports).
The clinical trial report must summarise and report important quality issues (including instances in which acceptable ranges are exceeded) and the remedial actions taken.
The sponsor must ensure the integrity and confidentiality of data generated and managed.
Quality control must be applied to relevant stages of data handling to ensure data are of sufficient quality to generate reliable results.
Quality assurance and quality control activities, including data review, must focus on data of higher criticality and relevant metadata.
Documented processes must be implemented to ensure data integrity for the full data life cycle.
Data changes made by the sponsor to data entered by the investigator or trial participants must be justified, agreed upon in advance by the investigator, and documented.
The sponsor must ensure the traceability of data transformations and derivations during data processing and analysis.
The rationale for exclusion of any participant (or particular data point) from any analysis set must be clearly described and documented.
Deviations from the planned statistical analysis or changes made to data after unblinding must be clearly documented and justified, occurring only in exceptional circumstances.
Post-unblinding data changes and deviations from planned statistical analyses must be reported in the clinical trial report.
Statistical programming records related to trial results reports, including quality control/validation activities, must be retained.

Statistical programming outputs must be traceable to statistical software programs, dated, time stamped, protected against changes, and have access controls to avoid inappropriate viewing of information that may introduce bias.
Clinical trial reports in marketing applications must meet ICH E3 standards or applicable regulatory requirements.
Consideration should be given to the coordinating investigator being a signatory on the clinical trial report (see ICH E3).
Summaries of trial results provided to participants must use non-technical, understandable, and non-promotional language.
The quality and amount of information generated in a clinical trial must be sufficient to address trial objectives, provide confidence in results, and support good decision making.
Systems and processes ensuring quality must be designed and implemented proportionately to risks to participants and reliability of trial results.
Any unplanned unblinding must be assessed for its impact on trial results, and appropriate actions taken.
Acquired data from any source, including directly captured data, must be accompanied by relevant metadata.
Audit trails, reports, and logs must be interpretable and support review.
Automatic capture of date and time of data entries or transfer must be unambiguous (e.g., coordinated universal time (UTC)).
Data corrections must be attributed, justified, supported by source records around the time of original entry, and performed in a timely manner.
Validated processes for electronic data transfer, exchange, and migration must ensure data integrity and confidentiality.
Data exchange/transfer or system migration processes must be documented for traceability, and data reconciliation implemented to avoid loss and unintended modifications.
Activities to finalise data sets prior to analysis must be confirmed and documented per pre-specified procedures.
Data extraction and determination of data analysis sets must follow planned statistical analysis and be documented.
Trial data and relevant metadata must be archived for retrieval and readability, protected from unauthorised access and alterations throughout retention.
Records must be identifiable and version controlled (when appropriate), including authors, reviewers, approvers, date, and signature (electronic or physical) where necessary.
The sponsor and investigator/institution must maintain a record of where essential records, including source records, are located.
Storage systems for trial records must provide appropriate identification, version history, search, and retrieval.
Essential records must be collected and filed in a timely manner.
Essential records must be retained in a way that ensures they remain complete, readable, readily available, and directly accessible upon request.
Alterations to essential records must be traceable.
Original records should generally be retained by the responsible party who generated them.
When a copy permanently replaces an original essential record, it must fulfill certified copy requirements.
The Investigator's Brochure (IB) must be reviewed at least annually and revised as necessary in compliance with sponsor's documented procedures.
IB information must be presented in a concise, simple, objective, balanced, and non-promotional form to enable unbiased risk-benefit assessment.
Submitted PDF files should be readable by Adobe Acrobat X.
Submitted PDF files should not require additional software or plug-ins to be read and navigated.
Submitted PDF files should be text searchable.
If plug-ins are used during the creation of a PDF document, prior to submitting the document, ensure that a plug-in is not needed for review or archive.
Ensure that all hypertext links in documents remain active after conversion to PDF/A.
The reviewer should be able to print, select text and graphics, and make changes to text, notes and form fields using the provided copy of the files, unless it's an FDA Form with inherent security.
Fully embed all non-standard fonts.
When fonts are embedded, all characters for the font should be included, not just a subset of the fonts being used in the document.
Inspect documents to make sure all non-standard fonts are fully embedded prior to submission.
When creating documents which include scanned images, ensure that any resizing of the image does not reduce the effective font size below the recommended size.
Any colors used should be tested prior to submission by printing sample pages from the document using a grayscale printer.
Save the page orientation for proper viewing and printing within the document.
Avoid image-based PDF files whenever possible.
If scanned files must be submitted, they should be made text searchable where possible.
If optical character recognition software is used, verify that imaged text is converted completely and accurately.
The use of grayscale and color should be used only when these features improve the reviewability of the material.
Compress files using either Zip/Plate or CCITT Group 4.
Create files from source documents using the “Optimize the PDF for fast web view” option to reduce file sizes and file opening times.
For printing, use CMYK (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Black) color model as opposed to the RGB model.
Pantone Matching using the color profile provided by CMYK ensure color consistency for printing.
The International Color Consortium (ICC) color profile specification is used when PDF documents are printed.
Include a hypertext linked Table of Contents (TOC) and bookmarks in documents 5 pages or longer.

Use hypertext links throughout the body of all documents to link to supporting annotations, related sections, references, appendices, tables or figures that are not located on the same page as the narrative text.
Hypertext links in text can be designated by rectangles using thin lines or by blue text.
A consistent method of designating links in a document avoids confusion.
Hypertext links that open a file or document should be set to open the file or document in a new window.
Using relative paths when creating hypertext links minimizes the loss of hyperlink functionality when submissions are loaded onto network servers.
The document TOC helps the reviewer navigate to the information of interest within the document that is not provided in the submission table of contents.

For documents with a table of contents, provide bookmarks and hypertext links for each item listed in the table of contents including all tables, figures, publications, other references, and appendices that are essential for navigation through documents.
The use of invisible rectangles and blue text in the table of contents for hypertext links avoids obscuring text.
Other help for navigation includes a bookmark hierarchy identical to the table of contents; up to four levels deep in the hierarchy.
When creating bookmarks and hyperlinks, set the magnification setting to "Inherit Zoom" so the destination page displays at the same magnification level used in the primary document.
Set the Navigation Tab to open to "Bookmarks Panel and Page" for initial document view.
If there are no bookmarks, set the Navigation Tab to "Page Only" for initial document view.
Page Layout and Magnification should be set to "Default" for initial document view.

It is easier to navigate through an electronic document if the page numbers for the document and the PDF file are the same, with the initial page of the document numbered as page one (with an exception for split documents).
For promotional materials, provide images at the highest resolution and depth practical.
For photographs in promotional materials, the image should be obtained with a resolution of at least 600 dpi.

For promotional materials, documents that are available only in paper should be scanned at resolutions that will ensure the pages are legible both on the computer monitor and when printed; at least 600 dpi is recommended.
Promotional material should be submitted according to its actual size when practical.
For promotional materials, when an image size is altered, the original dimensions must be stated.
For promotional materials, images of three-dimensional promotional pieces must show all sides and components.
For promotional materials, screenshots of websites submitted in PDF format should contain links that allow the reviewer to click on them to simulate navigation in the actual website.

For promotional materials, dynamic content such as audio, video, special effects, animations, attachments or 3D content are permitted if embedded in the PDF and playable via Adobe Acrobat X without the need for plug-ins or other special software.
Forbidden_Actions: Do not derive the integrated full report of a study by simply joining a separate clinical and statistical report.
Do not allow an excess of data to lead to overuse of symbols instead of words or easily understood abbreviations or to too small displays.
Do not simply repeat the description of results nor introduce new results in the Discussion and Overall Conclusions section.
Do not draw conclusions based only on patients who completed the study, ignoring dropouts.

Do not ignore potential bias introduced by a large number of dropouts, particularly if there are more early dropouts in one treatment group or the reasons for dropping out are treatment or outcome related.
Do not omit adverse events from tables based on causality assessment, even if considered not drug related or representing intercurrent illnesses.
Do not include persons with only an occasional role (e.g., an on-call physician, temporary substitute) in the list of investigators and important participants.
Do not unnecessarily exclude particular participant populations when designing a clinical trial.
Do not interpret the absence of expression of agreement or disagreement from a minor as assent.
Do not coerce or unduly influence a participant to participate or to continue their participation in the trial.

Do not include any language in the informed consent process that causes the participant to waive or to appear to waive any legal rights, or that releases or appears to release the investigator, the institution, the sponsor or their service providers from liability for negligence.
Do not interfere with the participant’s decision to refuse or withdraw participation at any time.
Do not place unnecessary burden on participants and investigators.
Do not have exclusive control of data captured in data acquisition tools in order to prevent undetectable changes.
Do not make changes to data entered by the investigator or trial participants unless justified, agreed upon in advance by the investigator and documented.
Do not routinely request audit reports from regulatory authorities to preserve the independence and value of the audit function.
Do not disable audit trails, reports and logs.

Do not modify audit trails except in rare circumstances (e.g., when a participant’s personal information is inadvertently included in the data) and only if a log of such action and justification is maintained.
Do not allow inappropriate viewing of information that may introduce bias in statistical programming records.
Do not allow data loss and unintended modifications during data transfer, exchange and migration.
Do not allow unauthorised access and alterations to archived trial data and relevant metadata throughout the retention period.
Do not allow unauthorised access, disclosure, dissemination or alteration and from inappropriate destruction or accidental loss of trial data.
Do not share data that may unblind the investigator.
Do not include JavaScript in PDF files.
Do not include dynamic content (audio, video, special effects, animations) in PDF files, with an exception for promotional materials.
Do not include attachments in PDF files, with an exception for promotional materials.
Do not include 3D content in PDF files, with an exception for promotional materials.
Do not include PDF annotations in documents, with an exception for promotional materials.
Do not activate security settings or password protection.
Do not add additional security to FDA Forms downloaded from the FDA Forms website.
Do not remove provided security settings from FDA Forms downloaded from the FDA Forms website.
Do not use font substitution if it can affect a document's appearance and structure, or the information conveyed by a document.
Do not use smaller point sizes than 9-10 for tables.
Do not use light font colors that do not print well on grayscale printers.
Do not allow header and footer information to invade the specified margins (i.e., header and footer information should not appear within 3/8 of an inch of the edge of the 8.5 by 11 inch page).
Do not resample scanned images to a lower resolution after scanning.
Do not subject a captured image to non-uniform scaling (i.e., sizing).
Do not embed thumbnails in PDF documents.
Do not use absolute links that reference specific drives when creating hypertext links.
Do not use links to root directories when creating hypertext links.
Do not use special characters in file names except hyphens and underscores.
Do not use punctuation, spaces, or other non-alphanumeric symbols in file names.
Formatting_Rules: Use charts and diagrams as needed.
Display the overall study design graphically with a flow chart, including timing of assessments.
Display graphically in a flow chart the frequency and timing of efficacy and safety measurements; visit numbers and times should be shown, or times alone can be used.
Abnormal laboratory values should be identified, e.g., by underlining, bracketing etc.
Graphs may be used for laboratory values over time.

A graph comparing the initial value and the on-treatment values of a laboratory measurement for each patient should locate the point defined by the initial value on the abscissa and a subsequent value on the ordinate.
Include patient identifiers for outliers in laboratory value graphs.
Individual patient profiles can be constructed in other formats, such as graphic displays.
Figures should be used to visually summarise important results or to clarify results that are not easily understood from tables.

References must be given in accordance with the internationally accepted standards of the 1979 Vancouver Declaration on 'Uniform Requirements for Manuscripts Submitted to Biomedical Journals' or the system used in 'Chemical Abstracts'.
P-values must be designated as one- or two-tailed.
PDF versions 1.4 through 1.7, PDF/A-1 and PDF/A-2 are acceptable for documents.
All non-standard fonts must be fully embedded.
Fonts should be restricted to the standard fonts listed in Table 1.
Use font sizes ranging from 9 to 12 point, with an exception for promotional materials.
Times New Roman 12-point font is recommended for narrative text.
Point sizes 9-10 are recommended for tables.
Ten point fonts are recommended for footnotes.
Black is the recommended font color, with an exception for promotional materials.
Blue can be used for hypertext links.
The page orientation must be saved for proper viewing and printing within the document.
Set up the print area for pages to fit on a sheet of paper that is 8.5 inches by 11 inches.
A margin of at least 3/4 of an inch on the left side of page is required.
A margin of at least 3/8 of an inch on the other sides (right, top, bottom) is sufficient.
For pages in landscape orientation, a margin of 3/4 of an inch at the top is required.
Oversized documents (e.g., CAD drawings or other specialized documents) and promotional materials submitted in PDF format should be created according to their actual page size.
Use 300 dots per inch (dpi) for scanning documents.
For photographs in promotional materials, the image should be obtained with a resolution of at least 600 dpi.
For promotional materials, documents that are available only in paper should be scanned at resolutions of at least 600 dpi.
File compression must use Zip/Plate or CCITT Group 4.
PDF documents do not need embedded thumbnails.
Documents 5 pages or longer must include a hypertext linked Table of Contents (TOC) and bookmarks.
Hypertext links in text can be designated by rectangles using thin lines or by blue text.
The bookmark hierarchy must be identical to the table of contents, up to four levels deep.
The magnification setting for bookmarks and hyperlinks must be set to "Inherit Zoom".
The Navigation Tab for initial document view must be set to "Bookmarks Panel and Page" (or "Page Only" if no bookmarks).
Page Layout and Magnification for initial document view must be set to "Default".
File names must use lower case characters.
File names must use hyphens and underscores.
For promotional materials, PDF restrictions for font size, color, and annotations stated in this document are not applicable.

Section_1.date_format_rule: ALL dates MUST use 'DD Month YYYY' (e.g., '29 April 2020', '02 September 2021'). Zero-pad single-digit days (e.g., '02' not '2'). Never use 'April 29, 2020', '2020-04-29', 'Apr 29, 2020', or '29/04/2020'.
Section_1.output_format_description: The title page is a structured form. Each entry has a LABEL on the left and a VALUE on the right. In markdown, render each entry as: '**Label:**\nValue'. Separate entries with a blank line. Multi-line values (e.g., investigator address, multiple signatories) use line breaks within the value. Do NOT use bullet lists, numbered lists, or tables.
Section_1.data_not_available_handling: If a field's data cannot be found via reasoning_search after at least 2 different search queries, write '[Data Not Available]'. Never invent or guess.

required_fields_in_order.label: Vaccine Name and\nCompound Number
required_fields_in_order.description: The investigational product name and compound number. Use the exact product name and compound ID from the study documents.
required_fields_in_order.example: BNT162 RNA-Based COVID-19 Vaccines, Compound Number: PF-07302048
required_fields_in_order.search_query: What is the investigational product name and compound number?

required_fields_in_order.label: Report Title
required_fields_in_order.description: The full report title: report type (Interim Report/Final Report), any update qualifier, the complete study title from the protocol including phase, design descriptors, objectives, product, and population.
required_fields_in_order.example: Interim Report – Adolescent 6-Month Update: A Phase 1/2/3, Placebo-Controlled, Randomized, Observer-Blind, Dose-Finding Study to Evaluate the Safety, Tolerability, Immunogenicity, and Efficacy of SARS-CoV-2 RNA Vaccine Candidates Against COVID-19 in Healthy Individuals
required_fields_in_order.search_query: What is the full study title from the protocol?

required_fields_in_order.label: Protocol Number
required_fields_in_order.description: The protocol identification code, prefixed with the word 'Protocol'.

required_fields_in_order.label: Sponsor
required_fields_in_order.description: The primary sponsor organization's full legal name.
required_fields_in_order.search_query: Who is the sponsor of this clinical study?

required_fields_in_order.label: Sponsor Agent
required_fields_in_order.description: The sponsor agent or CRO, if applicable. If not applicable, omit this field.
required_fields_in_order.search_query: Who is the sponsor agent or CRO for this study?

required_fields_in_order.label: Phase of Development
required_fields_in_order.search_query: What phase is this clinical trial?

required_fields_in_order.label: First Subject First Visit
required_fields_in_order.description: Date of first subject enrolled in DD Month YYYY format. If distinct sub-populations have different start dates, list each with a parenthetical label separated by semicolon.
required_fields_in_order.search_query: When was the first subject first visit or first patient enrolled?

required_fields_in_order.label: Primary Completion Date
required_fields_in_order.description: The primary completion date in DD Month YYYY format, or 'Not applicable' if not yet reached.
required_fields_in_order.search_query: What is the primary completion date of this study?

required_fields_in_order.label: Data Cutoff Date
required_fields_in_order.description: The data cutoff date for this report in DD Month YYYY format.
required_fields_in_order.search_query: What is the data cutoff date for this report?

required_fields_in_order.label: Name and Affiliation of\nCoordinating/Leading\nInvestigator
required_fields_in_order.description: Full name with credentials (e.g., MD, PhD), institution name, complete postal address (street, suite, city, state, zip). Then a separate paragraph noting where the full investigator list is provided (e.g., Appendix 16.1.4).
required_fields_in_order.example:
Stephen Thomas, MD
SUNY Upstate Medical University
725 Irving Ave, Ste. 311
Syracuse, NY 13210

The names of the principal investigators, site addresses, and number of participants enrolled at each site are provided in the appendix titled List and Description of Investigators and Other Service Providers, Appendix 16.1.4.
required_fields_in_order.search_query: Who is the coordinating investigator or principal investigator and what is their institution and address?

required_fields_in_order.label: Sponsor's Signatories
required_fields_in_order.description: All sponsor signatories, each on their own line(s). For each: full name with degree(s), then title and organization on the next line. If multiple sponsors, group signatories by organization.
required_fields_in_order.example:
John L. Perez, MD, MBA, MA
Vice President, Vaccines Clinical Research and Development, Pfizer Inc

Kenneth Koury, PhD
Clinical Biostatistics Head, Vaccines Clinical Research and Development, Pfizer Inc

Eleni Lagkadinou, MD, PhD
Vice President, Clinical Development, BioNTech SE
required_fields_in_order.search_query: Who are the sponsor signatories for this clinical study report with their titles and degrees?

required_fields_in_order.label: Internal Reports\nReferenced
required_fields_in_order.description: List all prior CSR reports from the same study protocol. Each on its own line: protocol number + report type, then 'dated DD Month YYYY'. If no prior reports exist, omit this field.
required_fields_in_order.example:
C4591001 Booster Interim CSR:
dated 23 August 2021

C4591001 6-Month Update Interim CSR:
dated 29 April 2021
required_fields_in_order.search_query: What earlier interim or final clinical study reports have been issued for this protocol?

required_fields_in_order.label: Date of Current Version
required_fields_in_order.description: Date of the current report version in DD Month YYYY format.
required_fields_in_order.search_query: What is the date of this clinical study report?

required_fields_in_order.label: Date(s) of Previous\nReport(s)
required_fields_in_order.description: Date(s) of any previous version(s) of this report in DD Month YYYY format. If no previous versions, omit this field.
required_fields_in_order.search_query: What are the dates of previous versions of this clinical study report?

gcp_statement.heading: ## GCP STATEMENT
gcp_statement.template: This study was conducted in compliance with Good Clinical Practice (GCP) guidelines and, where applicable, local country regulations relevant to the use of new therapeutic agents in the country/countries of conduct, including the archiving of essential documents.

data_dependencies.title: Title page, protocol header, report cover page
data_dependencies.content_summary: Search for the title page, cover page, or first pages of the CSR and the protocol to extract: study title, product name, compound number, protocol number, sponsor, sponsor agent, phase, and all key dates.

data_dependencies.title: Coordinating/Principal Investigator name, institution, address
data_dependencies.content_summary: Search for the coordinating investigator or principal investigator details including name, credentials, institution, and full postal address.

data_dependencies.title: Sponsor signatories for the CSR
data_dependencies.content_summary: Search for the sponsor's signatories — the persons responsible for the study report within the sponsor company. Find their names, degrees, titles, and organizations.

data_dependencies.title: Previously issued CSRs or interim reports for this protocol
data_dependencies.content_summary: Search for any internal reports referenced or earlier CSRs from the same protocol, with their types and dates.

critical_safety_checks: ALL dates must use DD Month YYYY format with zero-padded day. No exceptions.
The GCP STATEMENT section must be present at the end.
Do NOT invent or hallucinate any data. Use '[Data Not Available]' if not found.
The study title and protocol number must exactly match the source documents.
Every required field must appear in the output in the specified order (unless explicitly conditional).
Signatories must include full name, degree(s), and title — do not abbreviate or omit credentials.

required_content: Provide a brief synopsis (usually limited to 3 pages) that summarises the study.
The synopsis must include numerical data to illustrate results, not just text or p-values.

data_dependencies.title: Synopsis (Example)
data_dependencies.content_summary: An example of a synopsis format used in Europe, illustrating the expected structure and content for a synopsis.

Section_3.section_title: Table of Contents for the Individual Clinical Study Report
required_content: Include the page number or other locating information of each section, including summary tables, figures and graphs.
Include a list and the locations of appendices, tabulations and any case report forms provided.

Section_4.section_title: List of Abbreviations and Definition of Terms
required_content: Provide a list of the abbreviations, and lists and definitions of specialised or unusual terms or measurements units used in the report.
Abbreviated terms must be spelled out and the abbreviation indicated in parentheses at first appearance in the text.
Confirm that the study and any amendments were reviewed by an Independent Ethics Committee or Institutional Review Board.
Confirm that the study was conducted in accordance with the ethical principles that have their origins in the Declaration of Helsinki.
Describe how and when informed consent was obtained in relation to patient enrolment (e.g., at allocation, pre-screening).

data_dependencies.title: List of IECs or IRBs (plus the name of the committee Chair if required by the regulatory authority) - Representative written information for patient and sample consent forms
data_dependencies.content_summary: A list of all IECs or IRBs consulted, and if required by the regulatory authority, the name of the committee Chair. Also, representative written information for the patient (if any) and a sample patient consent form.

critical_safety_checks: Confirm that the study and any amendments were reviewed by an Independent Ethics Committee or Institutional Review Board.
Confirm that the study was conducted in accordance with the ethical principles that have their origins in the Declaration of Helsinki.

required_content: Describe briefly the administrative structure of the study (e.g., principal investigator, coordinating investigator, steering committee, administration, monitoring and evaluation committees, institutions, statistician, central laboratory facilities, contract research organisation (C.R.O.), clinical trial supply management) in the body of the report.

The listing should include: a) Investigators, b) Any other person carrying out observations of primary or other major efficacy variables, such as a nurse, physician's assistant, clinical psychologist, clinical pharmacist, or house staff physician, c) The author(s) of the report, including the responsible biostatistician(s).

data_dependencies.title: List and description of investigators and other important participants in the study, including brief (1 page) CVs or equivalent summaries of training and experience relevant to the performance of the clinical study
data_dependencies.content_summary: A list of the investigators with their affiliations, their role in the study and their qualifications (curriculum vitae or equivalent). A similar list for other persons whose participation materially affected the conduct of the study.

data_dependencies.title: Signatures of principal or coordinating investigator(s) or sponsor's responsible medical officer, depending on the regulatory authority's requirement
data_dependencies.content_summary: Signatures of the principal or coordinating investigators if required by regulatory authorities, or the signature of the sponsor's responsible medical officer if not required.

data_dependencies.title: Principal or Coordinating Investigator(s) Signature(s) or Sponsor's Responsible Medical Officer (Example)
data_dependencies.content_summary: A sample form for the signatures of principal or coordinating investigator(s) or sponsor's responsible medical officer.

required_content: The introduction should contain a brief statement (maximum: 1 page) placing the study in the context of the development of the test drug/investigational product, relating the critical features of the study (e.g., rationale and aims, target population, treatment, duration, primary endpoints) to that development.

Identify or describe any guidelines that were followed in the development of the protocol or any other agreements/meetings between the sponsor/company and regulatory authorities that are relevant to the particular study.
Provide a statement describing the overall purpose(s) of the study.
Describe briefly but clearly the overall study plan and design (configuration) of the study (e.g., parallel, cross-over), using charts and diagrams as needed.
If other studies used a very similar protocol, note this and describe any important differences.
If any information in this section comes from sources other than the protocol, these should be identified.

The information provided should include: treatments studied (specific drugs, doses and procedures); patient population studied and the number of patients to be included; level and method of blinding/masking (e.g., open, double-blind, single-blind, blinded evaluators and unblinded patients and/or investigators); kind of control(s) (e.g., placebo, no treatment, active drug, dose-response, historical) and study configuration (parallel, cross-over); method of assignment to treatment (randomisation, stratification); sequence and duration of all study periods, including pre-randomisation and post-treatment periods, therapy withdrawal periods and single- and double-blind treatment periods.
Specify when patients are randomised.
Include any safety, data monitoring or special steering or evaluation committees.
Include any interim analyses.
Discuss the specific control chosen and the study design used, as necessary.
If randomisation was not used, explain how other techniques, if any, guarded against systematic selection bias.
Discuss known or potential problems associated with the study design or control group chosen, in light of the specific disease and therapies being studied.
For a crossover design, consider, among other things, the likelihood of spontaneous change in the disease and of carry-over effects of treatment during the study.
If efficacy was to be demonstrated by showing equivalence, address problems associated with such study designs.
Provide a basis for considering the study capable of distinguishing active from inactive therapy.
Discuss how to assess the ability of the present study to distinguish effective from ineffective therapy.
Discuss the degree of inferiority of the therapy (often referred to as the delta value) the study was intended to show was not exceeded.
Give particular attention to the limitations of historical controls.
Discuss other specific features of the design, including presence or absence of washout periods and the duration of the treatment period, especially for a chronic illness.
Explain the rationale for dose and dose-interval selection, if it is not obvious.
Describe procedures used to seek evidence of 'escape' from drug effect at the end of the dose-interval, such as measurements of effect just prior to dosing.
In a parallel design dose-response study, explain the choice of doses.
Describe the patient population and the selection criteria used to enter the patients into the study, and discuss the suitability of the population for the purposes of the study.

Present specific diagnostic criteria used, as well as specific disease requirements (e.g., disease of a particular severity or duration, results of a particular test or rating scale(s) or physical examination, particular features of clinical history, such as failure or success on prior therapy, or other potential prognostic factors and any age, sex or ethnic factors).
Describe screening criteria and any additional criteria for randomisation or entry into the test drug/investigational product treatment part of the trial.
If there is reason to believe that there were additional entry criteria, not defined in the protocol, discuss the implications of these.
Specify the criteria for exclusion at entry into the study and provide the rationale (e.g., safety concerns, administrative reasons or lack of suitability for the trial).
Describe the predetermined reasons for removing patients from therapy or assessment observation, if any, as should the nature and duration of any planned follow-up observations in those patients.

Describe the precise treatments or diagnostic agents to be administered in each arm of the study, and for each period of the study, including route and mode of administration, dose and dosage schedule.
In the text of the report, give a brief description of the test drug(s)/investigational product(s) (formulation, strength, batch number(s)).
Provide the source of placebos and active control/comparator product(s).
Note any modification of comparator product(s) from their usual commercial state, and describe the steps taken to assure that their bioavailability was unaltered.
For long-duration trials of investigational products with limited shelf-lives or incomplete stability data, describe the logistics of resupply of the materials.
Note any use of test materials past their expiry date, and identify patients receiving them.
If there were specific storage requirements, these should also be described.

Describe the specific methods used to assign patients to treatment groups, e.g., centralised allocation, allocation within sites, adaptive allocation (that is, assignment on the basis of earlier assignment or outcome) in the text of the report, including any stratification or blocking procedures.
Explain any unusual features of assignment methods.
For a historically controlled trial, explain how the particular control was selected and what other historical experiences were examined, if any, and how their results compared to the control used.
Give the doses or dose ranges used in the study for all treatments and describe the basis for choosing them (e.g., prior experience in humans, animal data).
Describe procedures for selecting each patient's dose of test drug/investigational product and active control/comparator.
Describe procedures for back-titration, if any.
Describe the timing (time of day, interval) of dosing and the relation of dosing to meals, and note if it was not specified.
Describe any specific instructions to patients about when or how to take the dose(s).
Provide a description of the specific procedures used to carry out blinding (e.g., how bottles were labelled, labels that reveal blind-breakage, sealed code list/envelopes, double dummy techniques).
Include the circumstances in which the blind would be broken for an individual or for all patients, e.g., for serious adverse events, the procedures used and who had access to patient codes.
If the study allowed for some investigators to remain unblinded (e.g., to allow them to adjust medication), explain the means of shielding other investigators.

Describe measures taken to ensure that test drug/investigational product and placebo were indistinguishable and evidence that they were indistinguishable, as should the appearance, shape, smell, and taste of the test material.
Describe measures to prevent unblinding by laboratory measurements, if used.
If there was a data monitoring committee with access to unblinded data, describe procedures to ensure maintenance of overall study blinding.
Explain the procedure to maintain the blinding when interim analyses are performed.
If blinding was considered unnecessary to reduce bias for some or all of the observations, explain this.
If blinding was considered desirable but not feasible, discuss the reasons and implications.
Identify problems or potential problems with imperfect blinding (e.g., obvious drug effects) and describe any attempts to assess the magnitude of the problem or manage it.

Describe which drugs or procedures were allowed before and during the study, whether and how their use was recorded, and any other specific rules and procedures related to permitted or forbidden concomitant therapy.
Discuss how allowed concomitant therapy might affect the outcome due either to drug-drug interaction or to direct effects on the study endpoints.
Explain how the independent effects of concomitant and study therapies could be ascertained.

Describe the measures taken to ensure and document treatment compliance, e.g., drug accountability, diary cards, blood, urine or other body fluid drug level measurements, or medication event monitoring.

Describe the specific efficacy and safety variables to be assessed and laboratory tests to be conducted, their schedule (days of study, time of day, relation to meals, and the timing of critical measures in relation to test drug administration, e.g., just prior to next dose, two hours after dose), the methods for measuring them, and the persons responsible for the measurements.
Report any changes in personnel carrying out critical measurements.
Note any specific instructions (e.g., guidance or use of a diary) to the patients.
