Explain in full any definitions used to characterise outcome (e.g., criteria for determining occurrence of acute myocardial infarction, designation of the location of the infarction, characterisation of a stroke as thrombotic or haemorrhagic, distinction between TIA and stroke, assignment of cause of death).
Describe any techniques used to standardise or compare results of laboratory tests or other clinical measurements (e.g., ECG, chest X-ray), especially in multicentre studies.
If anyone other than the investigator was responsible for evaluation of clinical outcomes (e.g., the sponsor or an external committee), identify the person or group.
Describe fully the procedures, including means of maintaining blindness, and centralising readings and measurements, for evaluation of clinical outcomes by parties other than the investigator.

Describe the means of obtaining adverse event data (volunteered, checklist, or, questioning), as should any specific rating scale(s) used and any specifically planned follow-up procedures for adverse events or any planned rechallenge procedure.
Describe any rating of adverse events by the investigator, sponsor or external group (e.g., rating by severity or, likelihood of drug causation).
Give the criteria for such ratings, if any, and clearly identify the parties responsible for the ratings.
If efficacy or safety was to be assessed in terms of categorical ratings, numerical scores etc., provide the criteria used for point assignment (e.g., definitions of point scores).
For multicentre studies, indicate how methods were standardised.
If any of the efficacy or safety assessments was not standard (i.e., widely used and generally recognised as reliable, accurate, and relevant), document its reliability, accuracy and relevance.
It may be helpful to describe alternatives considered but rejected for non-standard assessments.

If a surrogate end point (a laboratory measurement or physical measurement or sign that is not a direct measure of clinical benefit) was used as a study end point, justify this e.g., by reference to clinical data, publications, guidelines or previous actions by regulatory authorities.
Clearly specify the primary measurements and endpoints used to determine efficacy.

If the protocol did not identify the primary variables, the study report should explain how these critical variables were selected (e.g., by reference to publications, guidelines or previous actions by regulatory authorities) and when they were identified (i.e., before or after the study was completed and unblinded).
If an efficacy threshold was defined in the protocol, describe this.
Describe any drug concentrations to be measured, and the sample collection times and periods in relation to the timing of drug administration.
Address any relation of drug administration and sampling to ingestion of food, posture and the possible effects of concomitant medication/alcohol/caffeine/nicotine.
Describe the biological sample measured, the handling of samples and the method of measurement used, referring to published and/or internal assay validation documentation for methodological details.
Where other factors are believed important in assessing pharmacokinetics (e.g., soluble circulating receptors, renal or hepatic function), specify the timing and plans to measure these factors.
Describe in brief the quality assurance and quality control systems implemented to assure the quality of the data.
If no quality assurance and quality control systems were used, state this.

Describe any steps taken at the investigation site or centrally to ensure the use of standard terminology and the collection of accurate, consistent, complete, and reliable data, such as training sessions, monitoring of investigators by sponsor personnel, instruction manuals, data verification, cross-checking, use of a central laboratory for certain tests, centralised ECG reading, or data audits.
Note whether investigator meetings or other steps were taken to prepare investigators and standardise performance.
If the sponsor used an independent internal or external auditing procedure, mention it here and describe it in appendix 16.1.8.
Describe the statistical analyses planned in the protocol and any changes made before outcome results were available.
In this section, emphasize which analyses, comparisons and statistical tests were planned, not on which ones were actually used.

If critical measurements were made more than once, specify the particular measurements (e.g., average of several measurements over the entire study, values at particular times, values only from study completers, or last on-therapy value) planned as the basis for comparison of test drug/investigational product and control.
If more than one analytical approach is plausible, e.g., changes from baseline response, slope analysis, life table analysis, identify the planned approach.
Specify whether the primary analysis is to include adjustment for covariates.
If there were any planned reasons for excluding from analysis patients for whom data are available, describe these.
If there were any subgroups whose results were to be examined separately, identify these.
If categorical responses (global scales, severity scores, responses of a certain size) were to be used in analysing responses, clearly define them.
Describe planned monitoring of the results of the study.
If there was a data monitoring committee, either within or outside the sponsor's control, describe its composition and operating procedures and give procedures to maintain study blinding.

Describe the frequency and nature of any planned interim analysis, any specified circumstances in which the study would be terminated and any statistical adjustments to be employed because of interim analyses.
Provide the planned sample size and the basis for it, such as statistical considerations or practical limitations.
Give methods for sample size calculation together with their derivations or source of reference.
Give estimates used in the calculations and provide explanations as to how they were obtained.
For a study intended to show a difference between treatments, specify the difference the study is designed to detect.

For a positive control study intended to show that a new therapy is at least as effective as the standard therapy, the sample size determination should specify the difference between treatments that would be considered unacceptably large and therefore the difference the study is designed to be able to exclude.

Describe any change in the conduct of the study or planned analyses (e.g., dropping a treatment group, changing the entry criteria or drug dosages, adjusting the sample size etc.) instituted after the start of the study.

Describe the time(s) and reason(s) for the change(s), the procedure used to decide on the change(s), the person(s) or group(s) responsible for the change(s) and the nature and content of the data available (and to whom they were available) when the change was made, whether the change was documented as a formal protocol amendment or not (Personnel changes need not be included).
Discuss briefly any possible implications of the change(s) for the interpretation of the study in this section and more fully in other appropriate sections of the report.
In every section of the report, make a clear distinction between conditions (procedures) planned in the protocol and amendments or additions.
It is particularly critical that the timing of changes relative to blind breaking and availability of outcome results be well characterised.

data_dependencies.title: Protocol and protocol amendments
data_dependencies.content_summary: The actual protocol and any changes made to it.

data_dependencies.title: Sample case report form (unique pages only)
data_dependencies.content_summary: Unique pages of a sample case report form; identical pages from forms for different evaluations or visits are not necessary.

data_dependencies.title: Study Design and Schedule of Assessments (Example)
data_dependencies.content_summary: An example flow chart displaying the study design and timing of assessments.

data_dependencies.title: Study Design and Schedule of Assessments (Example)
data_dependencies.content_summary: An example flow chart displaying the study design and timing of assessments.

data_dependencies.title: Listing of patients receiving test drug(s)/investigational product(s) from specific batches, where more than one batch was used
data_dependencies.content_summary: A listing identifying patients who received test drug(s)/investigational product(s) from specific batches, if more than one batch was used.

data_dependencies.title: Randomisation scheme and codes (patient identification and treatment assigned)
data_dependencies.content_summary: A detailed description of the randomisation method, including how it was executed, with references if necessary. A table exhibiting the randomisation codes, patient identifier, and treatment assigned, by centre for multicentre studies. Explanation of the method of generating random numbers.

data_dependencies.title: Study Design and Schedule of Assessments (Example)
data_dependencies.content_summary: An example flow chart displaying the frequency and timing of efficacy and safety measurements.

data_dependencies.title: Documentation of inter-laboratory standardisation methods and quality assurance procedures if used
data_dependencies.content_summary: Documentation of methods and procedures used for inter-laboratory standardisation and quality assurance.

data_dependencies.title: Audit certificates (if available) (see Annex IVa and IVb of the guideline)
data_dependencies.content_summary: Description of any independent internal or external auditing procedure used by the sponsor, and audit certificates if available.

data_dependencies.title: Documentation of statistical methods
data_dependencies.content_summary: Detailed documentation of statistical methods. Includes minutes of meetings of any data monitoring group and any data reports reviewed at those meetings, particularly a meeting that led to a change in the protocol or early termination of the study.

critical_safety_checks: Specify the criteria for exclusion at entry into the study and provide the rationale (e.g., safety concerns, administrative reasons or lack of suitability for the trial).
Note any use of test materials past their expiry date, and identify patients receiving them.
If there was a data monitoring committee with access to unblinded data, describe procedures to ensure maintenance of overall study blinding.
Explain the procedure to maintain the blinding when interim analyses are performed.
Identify problems or potential problems with imperfect blinding (e.g., obvious drug effects) and describe any attempts to assess the magnitude of the problem or manage it.
Describe the specific efficacy and safety variables to be assessed and laboratory tests to be conducted.

Describe the means of obtaining adverse event data (volunteered, checklist, or, questioning), as should any specific rating scale(s) used and any specifically planned follow-up procedures for adverse events or any planned rechallenge procedure.
Describe any rating of adverse events by the investigator, sponsor or external group (e.g., rating by severity or, likelihood of drug causation).
Give the criteria for such ratings, if any, and clearly identify the parties responsible for the ratings.

required_content: Provide a clear accounting of all patients who entered the study, using figures or tables in the text of the report.
Provide the numbers of patients who were randomised, and who entered and completed each phase of the study (or each week/month of the study).
Provide the reasons for all post-randomisation discontinuations, grouped by treatment and by major reason (lost to follow-up, adverse event, poor compliance etc.).

It may also be relevant to provide the number of patients screened for inclusion and a breakdown of the reasons for excluding patients during screening, if this could help clarify the appropriate patient population for eventual drug use.
Make clear whether patients are followed for the duration of the study, even if drug is discontinued.
Describe all important deviations related to study inclusion or exclusion criteria, conduct of the trial, patient management or patient assessment.

In the body of the text, protocol deviations should be appropriately summarised by centre and grouped into different categories, such as: those who entered the study even though they did not satisfy the entry criteria; those who developed withdrawal criteria during the study but were not withdrawn; those who received the wrong treatment or incorrect dose; those who received an excluded concomitant treatment.

data_dependencies.title: Disposition of Patients (Example)
data_dependencies.content_summary: An example flow chart illustrating the disposition of patients in a study, including numbers randomized, completed, and withdrawn with reasons.

data_dependencies.title: Disposition of Patients (Example)
data_dependencies.content_summary: An example flow chart illustrating the disposition of patients in a study, including numbers randomized, completed, and withdrawn with reasons.

data_dependencies.title: Discontinued patients
data_dependencies.content_summary: A listing of all patients discontinued from the study after enrolment, broken down by centre and treatment group, giving a patient identifier, the specific reason for discontinuation, the treatment (drug and dose), cumulative dose (where appropriate), and the duration of treatment before discontinuation. It should also note whether or not the blind for the patient was broken at the time of discontinuation. May include critical demographic data, concomitant medication, and major response variable(s) at termination.

data_dependencies.title: Listing of Patients Who Discontinued Therapy (Example)
data_dependencies.content_summary: An example listing format for patients who discontinued therapy, including patient identifier, reason for discontinuation, treatment, cumulative dose, duration of treatment, and whether the blind was broken.

data_dependencies.title: Protocol deviations
data_dependencies.content_summary: A listing of individual patients with protocol deviations, broken down by centre for multicentre studies.

critical_safety_checks: Note whether or not the blind for the patient was broken at the time of discontinuation.

required_content: Precisely define which patients were included in each efficacy analysis (e.g., all patients receiving any test drugs/investigational products, all patients with any efficacy observation or with a certain minimum number of observations, only patients completing the trial, all patients with an observation during a particular time window, only patients with a specified degree of compliance etc.).
If not defined in the study protocol, clearly state when (relative to study unblinding) and how inclusion/exclusion criteria for the data sets analysed were developed.

Generally, even if the applicant's proposed primary analysis is based on a reduced subset of the patients with data, there should also be for any trial intended to establish efficacy an additional analysis using all randomised (or otherwise entered) patients with any on-treatment data.
Present group data for the critical demographic and baseline characteristics of the patients, as well as other factors arising during the study that could affect response, in this section.
The data for the patient sample included in the 'all patients with data' analysis should be given first.

This can then be followed by data on other groups used in principal analyses, such as the 'per-protocol' analysis or other analyses, e.g., groups defined by compliance, concomitant disease/therapy, or demographic/baseline characteristics.
When such groups are used, data for the complementary excluded group should also be shown.
In a multicentre study where appropriate, comparability should be assessed by centre, and centres should be compared.
Provide a diagram showing the relationship between the entire sample and any other analysis groups.

The critical variables will usually include: demographic variables (age, sex, race), disease factors (specific entry criteria, duration, stage and severity of disease and other clinical classifications and sub-groupings in common usage or of known prognostic significance, baseline values for critical clinical measurements, concomitant illness at trial initiation, relevant previous illness, relevant previous treatment for illness treated in the study, concomitant treatment maintained or stopped at entry), other factors that might affect response to therapy (e.g., weight, renin status, antibody levels, metabolic status), and other possibly relevant variables (e.g., smoking, alcohol intake, special diets, and, for women, menstrual status and date of last menstrual period, if pertinent).
Summarise any measurements of compliance of individual patients with the treatment regimen under study and drug concentrations in body fluids, analysed by treatment group and time interval.

Compare treatment groups for all critical measures of efficacy (primary and secondary end-points; any pharmacodynamic end points studied), as well as benefit/risk assessment(s) in each patient where these are utilised.
In general, the results of all analyses contemplated in the protocol and an analysis including all patients with on-study data should be performed in studies intended to establish efficacy.
The analysis must show the size (point estimate) of the difference between the treatments, the associated confidence interval, and where utilised, the results of hypothesis testing.
Present analyses based on continuous variables (e.g., mean blood pressure or depression scale score) and categorical responses (e.g., cure of an infection) if both were planned and are available.
If categories are newly created (i.e., not in the statistical plan), explain the basis for them.
Even if one variable receives primary attention, other reasonable measures should be assessed, at least briefly.
Describe the time course of response, if possible.

For a multicentre study, where appropriate, include data display and analysis of individual centres for critical variables to give a clear picture of the results at each site, especially the larger sites.

If any critical measurements or assessments of efficacy or safety outcomes were made by more than one party, overall differences between the ratings must be shown, and each patient having disparate assessments must be identified.
The assessments used must be clear in all analyses.
Describe the statistical analysis used for clinical and statistical reviewers in the text of the report.

Discuss important features of the analysis including the particular methods used, adjustments made for demographic or baseline measurements or concomitant therapy, handling of drop-outs and missing data, adjustments for multiple comparisons, special analyses of multicentre studies, and adjustments for interim analyses.
Identify any changes in the analysis made after blind-breaking.
Explain the selection of, and adjustments for, demographic or baseline measurements, concomitant therapy, or any other covariate or prognostic factor in the report.
If the covariates or methods used in these analyses differed from those planned in the protocol, explain the differences and where possible and relevant, present the results of planned analyses.

Assess the results of a clinical trial not only for the subset of patients who completed the study, but also for the entire patient population as randomised or at least for all those with any on-study measurements.

Consider and compare several factors for the treatment groups in analysing the effects of dropouts: the reasons for the dropouts, the time to dropout, and the proportion of dropouts among treatment groups at various time points.
Describe procedures for dealing with missing data, e.g., use of estimated or derived data.
Provide detailed explanation as to how such estimations or derivations were done and what underlying assumptions were made.

Describe in full all interim analyses, formal or informal, pre-planned or ad hoc, by any study participant, sponsor staff member, or data monitoring group, even if the treatment groups were not identified.
Address the need for statistical adjustment because of such analyses.
Describe any operating instructions or procedures used for such analyses.
Describe data monitoring without code-breaking, even if this kind of monitoring is considered to cause no increase in type I error.
Present individual centre results where appropriate, e.g., when the centres have sufficient numbers of patients to make such analysis potentially valuable.
Explore the possibility of qualitative or quantitative treatment-by-centre interaction.
Note and discuss any extreme or opposite results among centres, considering such possibilities as differences in study conduct, patient characteristics, or clinical settings.
Treatment comparison must include analyses that allow for centre differences with respect to response.
If appropriate, present demographic, baseline, and post-baseline data, as well as efficacy data, by centre, even though the combined analysis is the primary one.

If there was more than one primary endpoint (outcome variable), more than one analysis of particular endpoint, or if there were multiple treatment groups, or subsets of the patient population being examined, the statistical analysis must reflect awareness of this and either explain the statistical adjustment used for type I error criteria or give reasons why it was considered unnecessary.
Devote particular attention to the effects of dropping patients with available data from analyses because of poor compliance, missed visits, ineligibility, or any other reason.
Carry out an analysis using all available data for all studies intended to establish efficacy, even if it is not the analysis proposed as the primary analysis by the applicant.
Any substantial differences resulting from the choice of patient population for analysis must be the subject of explicit discussion.

If an active control study is intended to show equivalence (i.e., lack of a difference greater than a specified size) between the test drug/investigational product and the active control/comparator, the analysis must show the confidence interval for the comparison between the two agents for critical end points and the relation of that interval to the prespecified degree of inferiority that would be considered unacceptable.

If the size of the study permits, examine important demographic or baseline value-defined subgroups for unusually large or small responses and present the results (e.g., comparison of effects by age, sex, or race, by severity or prognostic groups, by history of prior treatment with a drug of the same class etc.).
If these analyses were not carried out because the study was too small, this must be noted.
Where there is a prior hypothesis of a differential effect in a particular subgroup, this hypothesis and its assessment must be part of the planned statistical analysis.
In addition to tables and graphs representing group data, present individual response data and other relevant study information in tables.

The study report must indicate what material is included as an appendix, what is in the more extensive archival case report tabulations, if required by the regulatory authority, and what is available on request.

For a controlled study in which critical efficacy measurements or assessments (e.g., blood or urine cultures, pulmonary function tests, angina frequency, or global evaluations) are repeated at intervals, the data listings accompanying the report should include, for each patient, a patient identifier, all measured or observed values of critical measurements, including baseline measurements, with notation of the time during the study (e.g., days on therapy and time of day, if relevant) when the measurements were made, the drug/dose at the time (if useful, given as mg/kg), any measurements of compliance, and any concomitant medications at the time of, or close to the time of, measurement or assessment.
If, aside from repeated assessments, the study included some overall responder vs non-responder evaluation(s) (bacteriologic cure or failure), it should also be included.

In addition to critical measurements, the tabulation should note whether the patient was included in the efficacy evaluation (and which evaluation, if more than one), provide patient compliance information, if collected, and a reference to the location of the case report form, if included.
Critical baseline information such as age, sex, weight, disease being treated (if more than one in study), and disease stage or severity, is also helpful.
The baseline values for critical measurements would ordinarily be included as zero time values for each efficacy measurement.
When the dose in each patient can vary, the actual doses received by patients must be shown and individual patient's doses must be tabulated.
Although studies not designed as dose-response studies may have limited ability to contribute dose-response information, the available data should be examined for whatever information they can yield.
In examining the dose response, it may be helpful to calculate dose as mg/kg body weight or mg/m² body surface.
Any apparent relationship between response and concomitant therapy and between response and past and/or concurrent illness should be described.
While individual patient data ordinarily can be displayed in tabular listings, it has on occasion been helpful to construct individual patient profiles in other formats, such as graphic displays.

These might, for example, show the value of (a) particular parameter(s) over time, the drug dose over the same period, and the times of particular events (e.g., an adverse event or change in concomitant therapy).
It may be helpful to use individual patient profiles if overall evaluation of individual responses is a critical part of the analysis.

Concise description of the important conclusions concerning efficacy, considering primary and secondary end points, pre-specified and alternative statistical approaches and results of exploratory analyses.

data_dependencies.title: Patients excluded from the efficacy analysis
data_dependencies.content_summary: A tabular listing of all patients, visits and observations excluded from the efficacy analysis. The reasons for exclusions should also be analysed for the whole treatment group over time.

data_dependencies.title: Listing of Patients and Observations Excluded from Efficacy Analysis
data_dependencies.content_summary: An example listing format for patients and observations excluded from efficacy analysis, including patient identifier, reason for exclusion, and treatment group.

data_dependencies.title: Number of Patients Excluded from Efficacy Analysis (Example)
data_dependencies.content_summary: An example table showing the number of patients excluded from efficacy analysis over time for a treatment group, with similar tables to be prepared for other treatment groups.

data_dependencies.title: Demographic Data
data_dependencies.content_summary: Summary figures and tables of demographic data.

data_dependencies.title: Demographic data
data_dependencies.content_summary: Relevant individual patient demographic and baseline data, including laboratory values, and all concomitant medication for all individual patients randomised, broken down by treatment and by centre for multicentre studies.

data_dependencies.title: Compliance and/or drug concentration data (if available)
data_dependencies.content_summary: Summarised and tabulated measurements of compliance of individual patients with the treatment regimen and drug concentrations in body fluids, analysed by treatment group and time interval.

data_dependencies.title: Documentation of statistical methods
data_dependencies.content_summary: Detailed documentation of statistical methods. Includes minutes of meetings of any data monitoring group and any data reports reviewed at those meetings, particularly a meeting that led to a change in the protocol or early termination of the study.

data_dependencies.title: Individual efficacy response data
data_dependencies.content_summary: Individual response data and other relevant study information, including patient identifier, all measured or observed values of critical measurements (including baseline), time during study, drug/dose at time, compliance measurements, concomitant medications, and overall responder evaluation if applicable.

critical_safety_checks: If any critical measurements or assessments of efficacy or safety outcomes were made by more than one party, overall differences between the ratings must be shown, and each patient having disparate assessments must be identified.
The assessments used must be clear in all analyses.

required_content: Examine the extent of exposure (dose, duration, number of patients) to determine the degree to which safety can be assessed from the study.

Identify, classify, compare for treatment groups, and analyse common adverse events and laboratory test changes for factors that may affect frequency of adverse reactions/events (e.g., time dependence, relation to demographic characteristics, relation to dose or drug concentration).

Identify serious adverse events and other significant adverse events, usually by close examination of patients who left the study prematurely because of an adverse event (whether or not identified as drug related) or who died.
Present summarised data, often using tables and graphical presentations in the main body of the report.
Present listings of individual patient data.
Present narrative statements of events of particular interest.
In all tabulations and analyses, events associated with both test drug and control treatment must be displayed.

Characterise the extent of exposure to test drugs/investigational products (and to active control and placebo) according to the number of patients exposed, the duration of exposure, and the dose to which they were exposed.

Duration of exposure to any dose can be expressed as a median or mean, but also describe the number of patients exposed for specified periods of time (e.g., one day or less, 2 days to one week, more than one week to one month, more than one month to 6 months etc.).

Break down the numbers exposed to test drug(s)/investigational product(s) for the various durations into age, sex, and racial subgroups, and any other pertinent subgroups (e.g., disease, disease severity, concurrent illness).

Give the mean or median dose used and the number of patients exposed to specified daily dose levels; the daily dose levels used could be the maximum dose for each patient, the dose with longest exposure for each patient, or the mean daily dose.
Provide combined dose-duration information, such as the numbers exposed for a given duration (e.g., at least one month) to the most common dose, the highest dose, the maximum recommended dose etc.
In some cases, cumulative dose might be pertinent.
Dosage may be given as the actual daily dose or on a mg/kg or mg/m² basis as appropriate.
Break down the numbers of patients exposed to various doses into age, sex, and racial subgroups, and any other pertinent subgroups.
Describe the overall adverse event experience in the study in a brief narrative, supported by more detailed tabulations and analyses.
In these tabulations and analyses, events associated with both the test drug and control treatment must be displayed.

Display all adverse events occurring after initiation of study treatments (including events likely to be related to the underlying disease or likely to represent concomitant illness, unless there is a prior agreement with the regulatory authority to consider specified events as disease related) in summary tables.
In most cases, it will also be useful to identify in such tables 'treatment emergent signs and symptoms' (TESS; those not seen at baseline and those that worsened even if present at baseline).
The tables must list each adverse event, the number of patients in each treatment group in whom the event occurred, and the rate of occurrence.
When treatments are cyclical, e.g., cancer chemotherapy, it may also be helpful to list results separately for each cycle.
Adverse events must be grouped by body system.
Each event may then be divided into defined severity categories (e.g., mild, moderate, severe) if these were used.

The tables may also divide the adverse events into those considered at least possibly related to drug use and those considered not related, or use some other causality scheme (e.g., unrelated or possibly, probably, or definitely related).

In addition to these complete tables provided in 14.3.1, an additional summary table comparing treatment and control groups, without the patient identifying numbers limited to relatively common adverse events (e.g., those in at least 1% of the treated group), should be provided in the body of the report.

In presenting adverse events, display both the original terms used by the investigator and attempt to group related events (i.e., events that probably represent the same phenomena) so that the true occurrence rate is not obscured.
Use the basic display of adverse event rates described in section 12.2.2 (and located in section 14.3.1) of the report, to compare rates in treatment and control groups.
For this analysis it may be helpful to combine the event severity categories and the causality categories, leading to a simpler side-by-side comparison of treatment groups.

If study size and design permit, it may be useful to examine the more common adverse events that seem to be drug related for relationship to dosage and to mg/kg or mg/m² dose, to dose regimen, to duration of treatment, to total dose, to demographic characteristics such as age, sex, race, to other baseline features such as renal status, to efficacy outcomes, and to drug concentration.
It may also be useful to examine time of onset and duration of adverse events.
A variety of additional analyses may be suggested by the study results or by the pharmacology of the test drug/investigational product.
If the studies are small and if the number of events is relatively small, it may be sufficient to limit analyses to a comparison of treatment and control.
Under certain circumstances, life table or similar analyses may be more informative than reporting of crude adverse event rates.
When treatments are cyclical, e.g., cancer chemotherapy, it may also be helpful to analyse results separately for each cycle.
List all adverse events for each patient, including the same event on several occasions, giving both preferred term and the original term used by the investigator.

The listing should be by investigator and by treatment group and should include: Patient identifier, Age, race, sex, weight (height, if relevant), Location of CRFs, if provided, The adverse event (preferred term, reported term), Duration of the adverse event, Severity (e.g., mild, moderate, severe), Seriousness (serious/non-serious), Action taken (none, dose reduced, treatment stopped, specific treatment instituted etc.), Outcome (e.g., CIOMS format), Causality assessment (e.g., related/not related), Date of onset or date of clinic visit at which the event was discovered, Timing of onset of the adverse event in relation to last dose of test drug/investigational product (when applicable), Study treatment at time of event or most recent study treatment taken, Test drug/investigational product dose in absolute amount, mg/kg or mg/m² at time of event, Drug concentration (if known), Duration of test drug/investigational product treatment, Concomitant treatment during study.
Clearly explain any abbreviations and codes at the beginning of the listing or, preferably, on each page.
List all deaths during the study, including the post treatment follow-up period, and deaths that resulted from a process that began during the study, by patient.
List all serious adverse events (other than death but including the serious adverse events temporally associated with or preceding the deaths).
The listing of serious adverse events should include laboratory abnormalities, abnormal vital signs and abnormal physical observations that were considered serious adverse events.

List marked haematological and other laboratory abnormalities (other than those meeting the definition of serious) and any events that led to an intervention, including withdrawal of test drug/investigational product treatment, dose reduction, or significant additional concomitant therapy, other than those reported as serious adverse events.

Provide brief narratives describing each death, each other serious adverse event, and those of the other significant adverse events that are judged to be of special interest because of clinical importance.
These narratives can be placed either in the text of the report or in section 14.3.3, depending on their number.
Events that were clearly unrelated to the test drug/investigational product may be omitted or described very briefly.

In general, the narrative should describe the following: the nature and intensity of event, the clinical course leading up to event, with an indication of timing relevant to test drug/investigational product administration; relevant laboratory measurements, whether the drug was stopped, and when; countermeasures; post mortem findings; investigator's opinion on causality, and sponsor's opinion on causality, if appropriate.

In addition, the following information should be included in narratives: Patient identifier, Age and sex of patient; general clinical condition of patient, if appropriate, Disease being treated (if the same for all patients this is not required) with duration (of current episode) of illness, Relevant concomitant/previous illnesses with details of occurrence/duration, Relevant concomitant/previous medication with details of dosage, Test drug/investigational product administered, drug dose, if this varied among patients, and length of time administered.

Assess the significance of the deaths, other serious adverse events and other significant adverse events leading to withdrawal, dose reduction or institution of concomitant therapy with respect to the safety of the test drug/investigational product.

When required by regulatory authorities, the results of all safety-related laboratory tests should be available in tabular listings, using a display where each row represents a patient visit at which a laboratory study was done, with patients grouped by investigator (if more than one) and treatment group, and columns include critical demographic data, drug dose data, and the results of the laboratory tests.
As not all tests can be displayed in a single table, they should be grouped logically (haematological tests, liver chemistries, electrolytes, urinalysis etc.).
For all regulatory authorities, there should be a by-patient listing of all abnormal laboratory values in section 14.3.4, using the format described above.

For laboratory abnormalities of special interest (abnormal laboratory values of potential clinical importance), it may also be useful to provide additional data, such as normal values before and after the abnormal value, and values of related laboratory tests.
In some cases, it may be desirable to exclude certain abnormal values from further analysis.
The necessary evaluation of laboratory values must in part be determined by the results seen, but, in general, the following analyses should be provided.

For each parameter at each time over the course of the study (e.g., at each visit) describe: the group mean or median values, the range of values, and the number of patients with abnormal values, or with abnormal values that are of a certain size (e.g., twice the upper limit of normal, 5 times the upper limit; choices should be explained).
Provide an analysis of individual patient changes by treatment group.

A variety of approaches may be used for individual patient changes, including: I. 'Shift tables' showing the number of patients who are low, normal, or high at baseline and then at selected time intervals. II. Tables showing the number or fraction of patients who had a change in parameter of a predetermined size at selected time intervals. III. A graph comparing the initial value and the on-treatment values of a laboratory measurement for each patient by locating the point defined by the initial value on the abscissa and a subsequent value on the ordinate.
Discuss clinically significant changes (defined by the applicant).
When toxicity grading scales are used (e.g., WHO, NCI), changes graded as severe should be discussed regardless of seriousness.
Provide an analysis of the clinically significant changes, together with a recapitulation of discontinuations due to laboratory measurements, for each parameter.
Vital signs, other physical findings, and other observations related to safety should be analysed and presented in a way similar to laboratory variables.

If there is evidence of a drug effect, identify any dose-response or drug concentration-response relationship or relationship to patient variables (e.g., disease, demographics, concomitant therapy) and describe the clinical relevance of the observation.

Review the overall safety evaluation of the test drug(s)/investigational product(s), with particular attention to events resulting in changes of dose or need for concomitant medication, serious adverse events, events resulting in withdrawal, and deaths.

Identify any patients or patient groups at increased risk and pay particular attention to potentially vulnerable patients who may be present in small numbers, e.g., children, pregnant women, frail elderly, people with marked abnormalities of drug metabolism or excretion etc.
Describe the implication of the safety evaluation for the possible uses of the drug.

data_dependencies.title: Compliance and/or drug concentration data (if available)
data_dependencies.content_summary: Summarised and tabulated measurements of compliance of individual patients with the treatment regimen and drug concentrations in body fluids, analysed by treatment group and time interval. May include drug concentration data helpful for correlation with adverse events or changes in laboratory variables.

data_dependencies.title: Displays of Adverse Events
data_dependencies.content_summary: Summary tables displaying all adverse events occurring after initiation of study treatments, including changes in vital signs and laboratory changes considered serious or significant adverse events. Tables should list each adverse event, number of patients, rate of occurrence, grouped by body system, and potentially by severity and causality. Patient identification for each event is important.

data_dependencies.title: Adverse event listings (each patient)
data_dependencies.content_summary: A listing of all adverse events for each patient, including the same event on several occasions, giving both preferred term and the original term used by the investigator. The listing should be by investigator and by treatment group and include detailed information about the event, patient, treatment, and causality.

data_dependencies.title: Listings of Deaths, Other Serious and Significant Adverse Events
data_dependencies.content_summary: Listings of all deaths, other serious adverse events (including those preceding deaths, laboratory abnormalities, abnormal vital signs, and physical observations considered serious), and other significant adverse events (marked haematological/laboratory abnormalities, events leading to intervention).

data_dependencies.title: Narratives of Deaths, Other Serious and Certain Other Significant Adverse Events
data_dependencies.content_summary: Brief narratives describing each death, each other serious adverse event, and other significant adverse events of special clinical importance. Narratives should detail the event, clinical course, timing, relevant measurements, actions taken, outcome, causality, and patient/treatment information.

data_dependencies.title: Listing of individual laboratory measurements by patient, when required by regulatory authorities
data_dependencies.content_summary: Tabular listings of all safety-related laboratory tests, where each row represents a patient visit, with patients grouped by investigator and treatment group, and columns include critical demographic data, drug dose data, and laboratory test results. Abnormal values should be identified.

data_dependencies.title: Abnormal Laboratory Value Listing (Each Patient)
data_dependencies.content_summary: A by-patient listing of all abnormal laboratory values, using a format similar to the individual laboratory measurements listing. May include additional data for abnormalities of special interest.

critical_safety_checks: It is assumed that all patients entered into treatment who received at least one dose of the treatment are included in the safety analysis; if not, an explanation must be provided.
Summary tables of adverse events must include changes in vital signs and any laboratory changes considered serious adverse events or other significant adverse events.
Even when a causality assessment is used, adverse event tables must include all adverse events, whether or not considered drug related, including events thought to represent intercurrent illnesses.
To enable analysis and evaluation of data in adverse event tables, each patient having each adverse event must be identified.
All deaths during the study, including the post treatment follow-up period, and deaths that resulted from a process that began during the study, must be listed by patient.
All serious adverse events (other than death but including the serious adverse events temporally associated with or preceding the deaths) must be listed.
The listing of serious adverse events must include laboratory abnormalities, abnormal vital signs and abnormal physical observations that were considered serious adverse events.

Marked haematological and other laboratory abnormalities (other than those meeting the definition of serious) and any events that led to an intervention (including withdrawal of test drug/investigational product treatment, dose reduction, or significant additional concomitant therapy) must be listed.

Provide brief narratives describing each death, each other serious adverse event, and those of the other significant adverse events that are judged to be of special interest because of clinical importance.

The significance of the deaths, other serious adverse events and other significant adverse events leading to withdrawal, dose reduction or institution of concomitant therapy must be assessed with respect to the safety of the test drug/investigational product.
Particular attention must be paid to whether any of these events may represent a previously unsuspected important adverse effect of the test drug/investigational product.

For serious adverse events that appear of particular importance, it may be useful to use life table or similar analyses to show their relation to time on test drug/investigational product and to assess their risk over time.
Abnormal laboratory values should be identified, e.g., by underlining, bracketing etc.

Any decisions to exclude certain abnormal laboratory values from further analysis must be clearly explained, and the complete list of values provided (or available to authorities on request) must identify every abnormal value.
Clinically significant changes (defined by the applicant) must be discussed.
When toxicity grading scales are used (e.g., WHO, NCI), changes graded as severe must be discussed regardless of seriousness.
An analysis of the clinically significant changes, together with a recapitulation of discontinuations due to laboratory measurements, must be provided for each parameter.

The significance of the changes and likely relation to the treatment must be assessed, e.g., by analysis of such features as relationship to dose, relationship to drug concentration, disappearance on continued therapy, positive dechallenge, positive rechallenge, and the nature of concomitant therapy.
Particular attention must be given to changes not evaluated as efficacy variables and to those considered to be adverse events.

The overall safety evaluation of the test drug(s)/investigational product(s) must be reviewed, with particular attention to events resulting in changes of dose or need for concomitant medication, serious adverse events, events resulting in withdrawal, and deaths.

Any patients or patient groups at increased risk must be identified and particular attention paid to potentially vulnerable patients who may be present in small numbers, e.g., children, pregnant women, frail elderly, people with marked abnormalities of drug metabolism or excretion etc.

required_content: Briefly summarise and discuss the efficacy and safety results of the study and the relationship of risks and benefit, referring to the tables, figures, and sections above as needed.
The presentation should not simply repeat the description of results nor introduce new results.
Clearly identify any new or unexpected findings, comment on their significance and discuss any potential problems such as inconsistencies between related measures.
Discuss the clinical relevance and importance of the results in the light of other existing data.
Identify any specific benefits or special precautions required for individual subjects or at-risk groups and any implications for the conduct of future studies.
Alternatively, such discussions may be reserved for summaries of safety and efficacy referring to the entire dossier (integrated summaries).
Discuss the impact of exclusions on the generalisability of the study (as per Section 9.3.2).
critical_safety_checks: Identify any specific benefits or special precautions required for individual subjects or at-risk groups and any implications for the conduct of future studies.

Section_14.section_title: Tables, Figures and Graphs Referred to But Not Included in the Text
required_content: Figures should be used to visually summarise the important results, or to clarify results that are not easily understood from tables.
Important demographic, efficacy and safety data should be presented in summary figures or tables in the text of the report.

If important demographic, efficacy and safety data become obtrusive because of size or number, they should be presented here, cross-referenced to the text, along with supportive, or additional, figures, tables or listings.
Summary figures and tables for demographic data may be presented in this section.
Summary figures and tables for efficacy data may be presented in this section.
Summary figures and tables for safety data may be presented in this section.
Displays of Adverse Events may be presented in this section.
Listings of Deaths, Other Serious and Significant Adverse Events may be presented in this section.
Narratives of Deaths, Other Serious and Certain Other Significant Adverse Events may be presented in this section.
Abnormal Laboratory Value Listing (Each Patient) may be presented in this section.
Provide a list of articles from the literature pertinent to the evaluation of the study.

References must be given in accordance with the internationally accepted standards of the 1979 Vancouver Declaration on 'Uniform Requirements for Manuscripts Submitted to Biomedical Journals' or the system used in 'Chemical Abstracts'.
